reasoning: 'Butterfly' is a rigid property (+R) because if an entity is a butterfly, it is essentially a butterfly in all possible worlds where it exists (it cannot cease to be a butterfly without ceasing to exist as that biological entity). It carries and supplies its own identity condition (+I, +O), allowing us to distinguish one butterfly from another and re-identify it over time. It exhibits unity (+U) because a butterfly is considered an intrinsic whole with a functional/organic structure. Finally, it is independent (-D) as it does not require the existence of another specific entity to exist.

'Red' is a property that can be applied to various entities and is not essential to any particular instance in all possible worlds, making it non-rigid (-R). It does not carry its own identity condition (-I) and does not supply its own identity (-O), as it is a quality that can be shared by many different types of entities. It is non-unifying (-U) because it does not inherently form a whole but rather is a characteristic that can be part of a larger whole. It is independent (-D) because the existence of the color red does not depend on the existence of any specific entity.

'Food' is a non-rigid property (-R) because an entity can cease to be food (e.g., through spoilage or transformation) without ceasing to exist. It carries an identity condition (+I) inherited from its more specific types (e.g., specific food items like apples or bread), but it does not supply its own unique identity condition separate from these specific types (-O). It is generally non-unifying (-U) because food can be composed of various parts and does not necessarily form a single, indivisible whole. It is independent (-D) as food items can exist without depending on the existence of another specific entity.

'Animal' is a rigid property (+R) because if an entity is an animal, it is essentially an animal in all possible worlds. It carries and supplies its own identity condition (+I, +O), allowing for the identification and re-identification of specific animals. It has unity (+U) because animals are considered intrinsic wholes with functional and organic structures. Finally, it is independent (-D) because the existence of an animal does not depend on the existence of another specific entity.

'RedApple' is a specialization of the sortal 'Apple' by the attribute 'Red'. It is non-rigid (-R) because an apple can change its color (e.g., from red to brown) without ceasing to be the same apple. It carries an identity condition (+I) inherited from 'Apple', but it does not supply its own unique identity condition (-O). As an object, it represents a whole (+U) and does not intrinsically depend on the existence of another specific entity to exist (-D).

'Group' is a non-rigid property (-R) because the individuals that constitute a group can change over time without the group ceasing to be the same group. It does not carry its own identity condition (-I) and does not supply its own identity condition either (-O), as the identity of the group is often determined by the identity of its members. It is typically a sum or aggregate of individuals (~U) rather than a unified whole. It is dependent (+D) because the group's existence relies on the existence of its members.

'Country' is a non-rigid property (-R) because a country can change its status (e.g., through dissolution or new formation) without the territory or people ceasing to exist. It carries identity (+I) and typically supplies its own identity condition (+O) based on political and geographical criteria. A country is considered a unifying entity (+U) because it is a coherent and integrated system with a government, territory, and population. It is dependent (+D) because its existence is often contingent on the recognition and interaction with other countries and the international community.

'AmountOfMatter' is a non-rigid property (-R) because the amount of matter can change (e.g., through processes like evaporation or combustion) without the entity ceasing to exist. It does not carry an identity condition of its own (-I) because the identity of the entity is not bound to a specific amount of matter. It does not supply its own identity condition (-O) and is typically considered a property of a more fundamental entity (like an object or substance). It lacks unity (-U) because it can be a part of different aggregates or wholes and is not necessarily a whole itself. It is dependent (+D) on the existence of a specific entity or substance to which the amount of matter pertains.

'PhysicalObject' is a rigid property (+R) because if something is a physical object, it remains a physical object in all possible worlds where it exists. It carries and supplies its own identity condition (+I, +O), allowing us to distinguish one physical object from another. It exhibits unity (+U) because physical objects are typically considered intrinsic wholes with a coherent structure. It is independent (-D) because a physical object can exist on its own without intrinsically depending on another specific entity.

'GroupOfPeople' is a non-rigid property (-R) because it is possible for a group to change its composition and still be considered the same group (e.g., people can join or leave). It carries an identity condition (+I) but does not supply its own unique identity condition (-O); the identity is often derived from the composition of individuals. A group of people is not a single intrinsic whole and is typically seen as a collection or aggregate (-U). It is independent (-D) because a group of people can exist without depending on the existence of a specific individual.

'LivingBeing' is a rigid property (+R) because if an entity is a living being, it cannot cease to be a living being without ceasing to exist in the relevant sense. It carries and supplies its own identity condition (+I, +O), allowing us to distinguish one living being from another and re-identify it over time. It exhibits unity (+U) because a living being is considered an intrinsic whole with a functional/organic structure. Finally, it is independent (-D) as it does not require the existence of another specific entity to exist.

'SocialEntity' refers to entities that exist by social convention or agreement. These entities are typically non-rigid (-R) because they can change their nature without ceasing to exist (e.g., a company can change its legal form). They carry identity (+I) and often supply their own identity conditions (+O), as they are defined by their social roles and structures. They are typically unifying (+U) because they are seen as cohesive units (e.g., a corporation, a club). They are dependent (+D) because their existence and continued recognition depend on social practices and institutions.

'Location' is a non-rigid property (-R) because an entity can change its location without ceasing to be that entity. It does not carry its own identity (+I), as the identity of a location is typically tied to the entities or features that define it (-O). Locations are often not considered unified wholes (-U) but rather are regions or points in space. They are dependent (+D) on the entities that define or occupy them.

'Fruit' is a rigid property (+R) because if an entity is a fruit, it is essentially a fruit in all possible worlds where it exists (it cannot cease to be a fruit without ceasing to exist as that biological entity). It carries identity (+I) but typically inherits its identity conditions from a more general sortal like 'Plant Product' or 'Edible Part' rather than supplying its own (-O). It possesses Unity (+U) because fruits are discrete biological entities (wholes). It is independent (-D) as its existence does not intrinsically depend on the existence of another specific individual (unlike a role or a part).

'Vertebrate' is a rigid property (+R) because if an entity is a vertebrate, it is essentially a vertebrate in all possible worlds where it exists (it cannot cease to be a vertebrate without ceasing to exist as that biological entity). It carries identity (+I) and typically inherits its identity conditions from more general sortals like 'Animal' or 'Living Being' rather than supplying its own (-O). It possesses unity (+U) because vertebrates are discrete biological individuals (wholes). It is independent (-D) as its existence does not intrinsically depend on the existence of another specific individual.

'Caterpillar' is a non-rigid property (-R) because a caterpillar can transform into a butterfly without ceasing to be the same organism. It carries an identity condition (+I) but inherits it from the broader category of 'Insect' or 'Organism' (-O). Caterpillars are unifying wholes (+U) as they are discrete biological entities with a coherent structure. They are independent (-D) as they do not intrinsically depend on another specific entity for their existence.

'Person' is a rigid property (+R) because being a person is essential to all instances of a person in all possible worlds. It carries and supplies its own identity condition (+I, +O), allowing for the distinction and re-identification of individuals. A person is a unified whole (+U) with a distinct and coherent structure. It is independent (-D) as a person does not intrinsically depend on the existence of another specific entity for its existence.

'LegalAgent' is a role that can be played by entities (typically persons or organizations) in legal contexts. It is anti-rigid (~R) because it is essential to the role that it is not essential to the underlying entity (e.g., a person can stop being a legal agent). It does not carry its own identity condition (-I) and does not supply its own identity condition (-O) because the identity is derived from the underlying entity. It is non-unifying (-U) because the role can be played by different entities at different times and does not form a single whole. It is dependent (+D) because the role of a legal agent is intrinsically tied to the legal system or context in which it operates.

'Entity' is a very broad and foundational concept that can be considered rigid (+R) because it is essential to all instances of entities in all possible worlds. It carries and supplies its own identity condition (+I, +O) as it is the most general form of individuation. It is unifying (+U) because it encompasses all things that can be considered wholes. It is independent (-D) because an entity can exist without depending on another specific entity.

'Organization' is a non-rigid property (-R) because an organization can change its legal status or dissolve without the members ceasing to be the same individuals. It carries identity (+I) and supplies its own identity condition (+O), allowing us to distinguish one organization from another. It is a unifying entity (+U) because it has a structured whole with a defined membership and purpose. However, it is dependent (+D) on the existence of its members and the social context in which it operates.

'Agent' is typically a role that can be played by various entities. It is anti-rigid (~R) because being an agent is not essential to the entities that can play this role (e.g., a person can be an agent, but they can also be in other roles). It does not carry its own identity condition (-I) and does not supply its own identity condition (-O), as the identity of an agent is derived from the underlying entity (e.g., a person). It is non-unifying (-U) because the concept of an agent does not necessarily imply a whole; it can apply to parts or aspects of entities. It is dependent (+D) because an agent's role is often defined in relation to actions or other entities.

'Apple' is a rigid property (+R) because if an entity is an apple, it cannot cease to be an apple without ceasing to exist as that specific fruit. It carries and supplies its own identity condition (+I, +O), allowing us to distinguish one apple from another and re-identify it over time. It exhibits unity (+U) because an apple is a discrete, whole object with a well-defined structure. Finally, it is independent (-D) as it does not require the existence of another specific entity to exist.
